content:
# Genesis Chapter 31 — The SOPHIA Bible (Consensus)
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Source Manuscript:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 17v–18v | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_031_page_001.jpg`, `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_031_page_002.jpg`, `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_031_page_003.jpg`
**Secondary Source:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), Pages 27–29 | **View LXX online:** https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0031 | https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0032 | https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0033

---

**Manuscript Confirmation:** The Leningrad Codex images (Folios 17v–18v, West Semitic Research Project, Bruce E. Zuckerman) have been read directly by all three council translators. Folio 17v (GEN_031_page_001.jpg) is photographed inverted per WSRP archival convention; once re-oriented, it carries the close of Genesis 30 and then Genesis 31 beginning at verse 1 through approximately verse 19 — covering Laban's sons' complaint, Jacob's changed perception of Laban, the LORD's command to return, Jacob's speech to Rachel and Leah recounting the dream, Rachel and Leah's response, Jacob's preparations, and Rachel's theft of the teraphim. Folio 18r (GEN_031_page_002.jpg) is in standard orientation and carries approximately verses 20–42: Jacob's flight, the notice of Laban's pursuit, God's dream-warning to Laban, the confrontation dialogue, Rachel's concealment of the teraphim, and Jacob's twenty-year speech culminating in the Pachad Yitschaq epithet. Folio 18v (GEN_031_page_003.jpg) is again photographed inverted; once re-oriented, it carries the covenant conclusion of Genesis 31 (vv. 43–55) and the opening of Genesis 32. The divine name יְהוָה (YHWH) is fully vocalized in v. 3; the term תְּרָפִים (teraphim) is legible in v. 19; the unusual epithet פַּחַד יִצְחָק (Pachad Yitschaq) is legible in vv. 42 and 53; the bilingual place-name verse (v. 47, both יְגַר שָׂהֲדוּתָא Aramaic and גַּלְעֵד Hebrew) is visible. Codex Vaticanus pages 27–29 carry the Greek LXX in continuous three-column uncial with chapter margin markers CAP. XXXIII, CAP. XXXIIII, and CAP. XXXVI. No Dead Sea Scrolls fragments of Genesis 31 are extant; the Leningrad Codex and Codex Vaticanus are the principal manuscript witnesses examined for this chapter.

---

*Laban's Sons — Changed Attitude — The LORD Commands Jacob to Return (vv. 1–3)*

**31:1** Now Jacob heard the words of Laban's sons, saying, "Jacob has taken all that was our father's, and from what was our father's he has made all this wealth."

**31:2** And Jacob saw the face of Laban — and behold, it was not toward him as before.

**31:3** Then the LORD said to Jacob, "Return to the land of your fathers and to your kindred, and I will be with you."

---

*Jacob Calls Rachel and Leah — His Speech Recounting Laban's Treatment (vv. 4–16)*

**31:4** So Jacob sent and called Rachel and Leah to the field, to his flock,

**31:5** and said to them, "I see that your father's face is not toward me as before. But the God of my father has been with me.

**31:6** You yourselves know that with all my strength I have served your father.

**31:7** Yet your father has cheated me and changed my wages ten times. But God has not permitted him to harm me.

**31:8** If he said, 'The speckled shall be your wages,' then all the flock bore speckled; and if he said, 'The striped shall be your wages,' then all the flock bore striped.

**31:9** Thus God has taken away the livestock of your father and given them to me.

**31:10** In the breeding season of the flock I lifted my eyes and saw in a dream — and behold, the male goats that were mounting the flock were striped, speckled, and dappled.

**31:11** Then the angel of God said to me in the dream, 'Jacob!' And I said, 'Here I am.'

**31:12** And he said, 'Lift your eyes and see — all the male goats mounting the flock are striped, speckled, and dappled, for I have seen all that Laban is doing to you.

**31:13** I am the God of Bethel, where you anointed a pillar and where you made a vow to me. Now arise, go out from this land and return to the land of your kindred.'"

**31:14** Then Rachel and Leah answered and said to him, "Is there still any portion or inheritance for us in our father's house?

**31:15** Are we not reckoned by him as foreigners? For he has sold us and has also entirely consumed our purchase price.

**31:16** For all the wealth that God has taken away from our father belongs to us and to our children. Now then, whatever God has said to you, do it."

---

*The Flight — Rachel Steals the Teraphim (vv. 17–21)*

**31:17** So Jacob arose and set his sons and his wives on the camels.

**31:18** And he drove away all his livestock and all his property that he had acquired — his acquired livestock that he had gained in Paddan-aram — to go to his father Isaac in the land of Canaan.

**31:19** Now Laban had gone to shear his sheep, and Rachel stole the teraphim that belonged to her father.

**31:20** And Jacob stole the heart of Laban the Aramean, in that he did not tell him that he was fleeing.

**31:21** So he fled with all that he had; he arose and crossed the River and set his face toward the hill country of Gilead.

---

*Laban Pursues — God Warns Him in a Dream (vv. 22–25)*

**31:22** On the third day, Laban was told that Jacob had fled.

**31:23** So he took his kinsmen with him and pursued him a journey of seven days, and overtook him in the hill country of Gilead.

**31:24** But God came to Laban the Aramean in a dream by night and said to him, "Take heed to yourself, lest you speak to Jacob either good or bad."

**31:25** And Laban overtook Jacob. Now Jacob had pitched his tent in the hill country, and Laban with his kinsmen had pitched in the hill country of Gilead.

---

*The Confrontation — Laban's Accusation and Jacob's Defense (vv. 26–42)*

**31:26** And Laban said to Jacob, "What have you done, that you have stolen my heart and driven away my daughters like captives taken by the sword?

**31:27** Why did you flee secretly and steal away from me, and not tell me? I would have sent you away with rejoicing and with songs, with tambourine and with lyre.

**31:28** And you did not allow me to kiss my sons and my daughters farewell. Now you have acted foolishly.

**31:29** It is in my power to do you harm. But the God of your father said to me last night, 'Take heed to yourself, lest you speak to Jacob either good or bad.'

**31:30** And now you have gone away because you longed greatly for your father's house — but why did you steal my gods?"

**31:31** Jacob answered and said to Laban, "Because I was afraid, for I said, 'Perhaps you would take your daughters from me by force.'

**31:32** With whomever you find your gods, he shall not live. Before our kinsmen, identify what is yours with me and take it." For Jacob did not know that Rachel had stolen them.

**31:33** So Laban went into Jacob's tent and into Leah's tent and into the tent of the two female servants, but he did not find them. And he went out of Leah's tent and entered Rachel's tent.

**31:34** Now Rachel had taken the teraphim and put them in the camel's saddle and sat on them. And Laban searched all the tent but did not find them.

**31:35** And she said to her father, "Let not my lord be displeased that I am unable to rise before you, for the way of women is upon me." So he searched but did not find the teraphim.

**31:36** Then Jacob was angry and quarreled with Laban. Jacob answered and said to Laban, "What is my transgression? What is my sin, that you have hotly pursued after me?

**31:37** For you have searched all my goods — what have you found of all your household goods? Set it here before my kinsmen and your kinsmen, and let them decide between us two.

**31:38** These twenty years I have been with you. Your ewes and your female goats have not miscarried, and the rams of your flock I have not eaten.

**31:39** What was torn by wild beasts I did not bring to you — I bore the loss of it myself. From my hand you required it, whether stolen by day or stolen by night.

**31:40** There I was: by day the heat consumed me, and the frost by night, and my sleep fled from my eyes.

**31:41** These twenty years I have been in your house. I served you fourteen years for your two daughters and six years for your flock, and you changed my wages ten times.

**31:42** If the God of my father, the God of Abraham and the Fear of Isaac, had not been with me, surely now you would have sent me away empty-handed. But God has seen my affliction and the toil of my hands, and he rebuked you last night."

---

*The Covenant at the Heap — Jegar-sahadutha and Gal-ed — Mizpah (vv. 43–55)*

**31:43** Then Laban answered and said to Jacob, "The daughters are my daughters, the sons are my sons, the flocks are my flocks, and all that you see is mine. But what can I do today for these my daughters or for the sons they have borne?

**31:44** Come now, let us make a covenant, you and I, and let it be a witness between you and me."

**31:45** So Jacob took a stone and set it up as a pillar.

**31:46** And Jacob said to his kinsmen, "Gather stones." So they took stones and made a heap, and they ate there upon the heap.

**31:47** Laban called it Jegar-sahadutha, but Jacob called it Gal-ed.

**31:48** And Laban said, "This heap is a witness between you and me today." Therefore its name was called Gal-ed,

**31:49** and also Mizpah, for he said, "May the LORD watch between you and me, when we are hidden from one another.

**31:50** If you afflict my daughters, or if you take wives besides my daughters — no man is with us — see, God is witness between you and me."

**31:51** Then Laban said to Jacob, "See this heap and see the pillar, which I have set between you and me.

**31:52** This heap is a witness and the pillar is a witness, that I will not pass over this heap to you, and you will not pass over this heap and this pillar to me, to do harm.

**31:53** The God of Abraham and the God of Nahor, the God of their father, judge between us." And Jacob swore by the Fear of his father Isaac,

**31:54** and Jacob offered a sacrifice on the mountain and called his kinsmen to eat bread. And they ate bread and spent the night on the mountain.

**31:55** Early in the morning Laban arose and kissed his grandchildren and his daughters and blessed them. Then Laban departed and returned to his place.

---

## SOPHIA's Council Notes

### The Architecture of Genesis 31

All three council translators identified the same three-movement structure of this chapter. LOGOS's structural summary is adopted by the council as the clearest formulation: (1) the departure from Paddan-aram (vv. 1–21), (2) the pursuit and confrontation (vv. 22–42), and (3) the covenant and farewell (vv. 43–55). Each movement is anchored by divine intervention: the LORD commands Jacob to return (v. 3); God warns Laban in a dream (v. 24); God is invoked as the unseen covenant witness at Mizpah (vv. 49–53). PNEUMA's characterization of the chapter's dramatic arc is also adopted: this is the chapter where Jacob finally runs, where Rachel and Leah reveal themselves as women who have their own clear-eyed grievances against their father, and where the confrontation at Gilead resolves into a wary, asymmetrical peace sealed by mutual surveillance under the divine eye.

---

### On 31:1 — "All This Wealth" (*kol-hakkavod hazzeh*)

EMET renders "all this glory" — preserving the full weight of *kavod* (glory, weight, honor, wealth). LOGOS renders "all this wealth." PNEUMA renders "all this wealth he has built up."

The Hebrew *כָּבוֹד* (*kavod*) carries both the sense of substantial material wealth and the honor/renown that attaches to it. EMET's note on the rhetorical punch of the sons' usage — calling Jacob's prosperity his "glory" in bitter irony — is well taken. The LXX Vaticanus reads *tēn doxan tautēn* ("this glory"), exactly preserving the *kavod* → *doxa* rendering.

The council adopts "all this wealth" (LOGOS) for the consensus translation. While "glory" is the more literally interesting rendering, "wealth" is the meaning the sons intend — they are not paying Jacob a theological compliment — and "wealth" reads naturally in English without requiring immediate annotation. EMET's full observation on the *kavod* wordplay is preserved in the council notes. The LXX support for "glory/doxa" is acknowledged and documented in the Disagreement Log.

---

### On 31:2 and 31:5 — "The Face of Laban" / "As Before"

All three translators preserve "the face of Laban" and the idiom "as before" (or "as formerly"). EMET renders "as formerly." LOGOS and PNEUMA render "as before." The Hebrew *כִּתְמוֹל שִׁלְשׁוֹם* ("as yesterday, as the day before yesterday") is the standard Hebrew idiom for "as in former times." The council adopts "as before" (LOGOS, PNEUMA) as the most natural English equivalent. The literal "as yesterday and the day before yesterday" is too cumbersome for a consensus translation; "as before" captures the idiom accurately. The repetition of the phrase in both v. 2 and v. 5 is deliberate narrative emphasis and is preserved in the consensus text.

---

### On 31:7 — "Ten Times" (*aseret monim*) ⚠️

EMET renders: "ten times." LOGOS renders: "ten times." PNEUMA renders: "ten times over — ten times!"

The Hebrew עֲשֶׂרֶת מֹנִים (*'aseret monim*) is literally "ten of times/counts" — the standard idiom for "ten times," likely functioning as a round number conveying systematic and repeated exploitation rather than a precise enumeration of ten discrete wage-changes. The LXX Vaticanus reads *deka amnas* ("ten female lambs") — a strikingly divergent reading that apparently understands *monim* as a livestock measure rather than a numeral multiplier. This is the most significant MT/LXX divergence in Genesis 31.

The council adopts "ten times" (EMET and LOGOS), which both preserves the literal number and allows its idiomatic force to register. PNEUMA's emphatic "ten times over — ten times!" adds rhetorical energy but departs from the narrative's register; the emphasis already belongs to the original repetition in v. 41. The LXX reading "ten female lambs" is documented in the Disagreement Log as a significant variant; the council follows the MT. The council's rendering of *aseret monim* is identical at v. 7 and v. 41.

---

### On 31:10 — "Dappled" (*berudim*)

EMET renders "dappled." LOGOS renders "spotted." PNEUMA renders "dappled."

The three marking-terms in the dream vision — *'aqudim* (striped), *nequdim* (speckled), *berudim* (dappled/spotted like hailstones) — should be rendered consistently with the same terms used across chapters 30–31. The council adopts "striped, speckled, and dappled" — "dappled" (EMET, PNEUMA) is the more precise rendering of *berudim* (literally "hail-spotted, mottled"), and "spotted" (LOGOS) risks confusion with *nequdim* in the same verse. This maintains the three-term vocabulary established in the Genesis 30 consensus.

---

### On 31:13 — "I Am the God of Bethel" (*ha-El Bet-El*) ⚠️

EMET renders: "I am the God of Bethel." LOGOS renders: "I am the God of Bethel." PNEUMA renders: "I am the God of Bethel."

All three translators agree on "the God of Bethel." The divine self-identification הָאֵל בֵּית-אֵל (*ha-El Bet-El*) — using the ancient singular *El* (not Elohim or YHWH) — is rendered consistently across all three versions. The LXX Vaticanus reads *egō eimi ho Theos ho ophtheis soi en topō Theou* ("I am the God who appeared to you in the place of God") — an interpretive paraphrase that renders "Bethel" as "the place of God" and adds a relative clause identifying the theophany. The council follows the MT's terse "the God of Bethel" (unanimous across EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA) rather than the LXX's expansion. EMET's note on the theological significance of *ha-El* (using the archaic singular El rather than Elohim) is adopted by the council as the definitive observation: this is the God of the specific Bethel theophany, calling in Jacob's vow from Genesis 28.

---

### On 31:15 — "Purchase Price" (the silver consumed by Laban)

EMET renders: "he has sold us and also eaten up our silver." LOGOS renders: "he has sold us and has also entirely consumed our purchase price." PNEUMA renders: "He sold us — he actually sold us — and then spent everything he got for us."

The Hebrew *וַיֹּאכַל גַּם אָכוֹל אֶת-כַּסְפֵּנוּ* ("he has also utterly eaten/consumed our silver") uses the cognate infinitive for emphasis ("consuming he has consumed"). The council adopts LOGOS's rendering "has also entirely consumed our purchase price" — "purchase price" makes the commercial situation clearer than the more literal "our silver," without straying from the text's meaning. PNEUMA's emphasis ("he actually sold us") is vivid but reflects a register shift toward rhetorical dramatization. EMET's "silver" is accurate but requires more contextual inference from the reader.

---

### On 31:19 — "Teraphim" (*hatteraphim*)

All three translators agree: "the teraphim." The term תְּרָפִים (*teraphim*) appears three times in this chapter (vv. 19, 34, 35) and is transliterated consistently by all three translators throughout. The LXX Vaticanus renders *ta eidōla* ("the idols") — a theologically loaded interpretive rendering. The council follows the unanimous position of EMET, LOGOS, and PNEUMA: "teraphim" is the correct rendering, retaining the Hebrew technical term for household cult objects rather than imposing the polemical "idols." Laban's own word for them in v. 30 ("my gods") is rendered "my gods" — preserving Laban's own characterization without editorial comment. The council is unanimous.

---

### On 31:20 — "Stole the Heart of Laban" (*wayyignov... et-lev*) ⚠️

EMET renders: "Jacob stole the heart of Laban the Aramean, in that he did not tell him that he was fleeing." LOGOS renders: identical to EMET. PNEUMA renders: "Jacob slipped away without telling Laban — he stole the heart of Laban the Aramean, giving him no warning that he was leaving."

The Hebrew idiom גָּנַב אֶת-לֵב (*ganav et-lev*, "to steal the heart") means to deceive by concealment — to act without the other party's knowledge. All three translators agree on preserving the literal idiom "stole the heart." The council adopts "Jacob stole the heart of Laban the Aramean, in that he did not tell him that he was fleeing" (EMET and LOGOS). PNEUMA's syntactic inversion (explaining the deception before stating the idiom) is more explanatory but dilutes the literary punch of the original's sequence. The LXX Vaticanus reads *eklepsen de Iakōb tēn kardian Labana* — exact rendering of the idiom, confirming it. EMET's note on the triple use of *ganav* (Rachel steals the teraphim, Jacob steals the heart, Laban later accuses Jacob "you stole my heart") is adopted as the council's definitive literary observation on this passage.

---

### On 31:24 and 31:29 — "Either Good or Bad" (*mittov 'ad-ra'*) ⚠️

EMET renders: "either good or evil." LOGOS renders: "either good or bad." PNEUMA renders: "not good, not bad."

The Hebrew merism מִטּוֹב עַד-רָע (*mittov 'ad-ra'*, "from good to evil") encompasses all speech whatsoever — God is prohibiting Laban from saying anything at all to Jacob. The LXX Vaticanus reads *apo tou kalou heōs tou ponērou* ("from the good to the evil") — exact literal rendering of the merism's structure.

The council adopts "either good or bad" (LOGOS) at both v. 24 and v. 29. "Either... or" captures the merism's mutually exclusive poles more naturally in English than EMET's "good or evil" (where "evil" in English implies moral wickedness more strongly than the Hebrew *ra'*, which means simply "bad/harmful") or PNEUMA's "not good, not bad" (which loses the merism's structural inclusivity). The council is substantially agreed; EMET's "evil" is the only minor dissent from "bad."

---

### On 31:26 — "Driven Away" / "Like Captives Taken by the Sword"

EMET renders: "drove away my daughters like captives of the sword." LOGOS renders: "carried away my daughters like captives taken by the sword." PNEUMA renders: "dragged my daughters away like prisoners of war."

The Hebrew *כִּשְׁבֻיוֹת חָרֶב* ("like captives of the sword") is Laban's rhetorical comparison. The council adopts "driven away my daughters like captives taken by the sword" — "driven away" (from *watten-naheg*, "you drove/led") is the more accurate rendering of the verbal action than LOGOS's "carried away" (which suggests a different physical mode of removal). PNEUMA's "prisoners of war" is a natural modern equivalent but loses the specific "sword" imagery of the Hebrew. The LXX Vaticanus reads *hōs aichmalōtidas makhaira* ("as captives by the sword") — confirming "sword."

---

### On 31:27 — "Steal Away from Me" (*vayyignov oti*)

EMET renders: "steal away from me." LOGOS renders: "steal away from me." PNEUMA renders: "cheat me."

The verb גָּנַב (*ganav*) appears here for the third time in Laban's accusation speech. The council preserves "steal away from me" (EMET and LOGOS) — this is the *ganav*-root, and maintaining it contributes to the chapter's deliberate wordplay around the root of theft and deception. PNEUMA's "cheat me" is a legitimate meaning-equivalent but severs the explicit verbal connection to Rachel's theft of the teraphim (v. 19) and Jacob's theft of Laban's heart (v. 20). The council notes: Laban uses "steal" (*ganav*) as the unifying accusation — and the reader is meant to feel the word's weight each time it falls.

---

### On 31:28 — "My Sons and My Daughters" (Laban's kiss)

EMET renders: "my sons and my daughters." LOGOS renders: "my sons and my daughters." PNEUMA renders: "my grandchildren and my daughters."

The Hebrew *לְבָנַי וְלִבְנֹתָי* ("my sons and my daughters") refers grammatically to Jacob's children (Laban's grandchildren) and Rachel and Leah. The council adopts "my sons and my daughters" (EMET and LOGOS) at v. 28, preserving the literal Hebrew — Laban uses the terms *bānay* and *bĕnotay*, which are his terms for Jacob's children in the same rhetorical frame that also claims ownership of everything ("the sons are my sons," v. 43). The "grandchildren" rendering at v. 55 (where Laban kisses them farewell) is addressed separately. The council's consistency is: at v. 28 (Laban's speech), "my sons and my daughters" follows the MT; at v. 55 (narrative description), contextual referents are clearer and "grandchildren" is preferred.

---

### On 31:35 — "The Way of Women" (*derekh nashim*) ⚠️

EMET renders: "the way of women is upon me." LOGOS renders: "the manner of women is upon me." PNEUMA renders: "I'm having my period."

The Hebrew דֶּרֶךְ נָשִׁים (*derekh nashim*, "the way of women") is the biblical euphemism for menstruation. All three translators agree on the meaning. The disagreement is whether to render the euphemism (EMET, LOGOS) or to translate the referent directly (PNEUMA).

The council adopts "the way of women is upon me" (EMET). This is the more precise and contextually resonant rendering: Rachel's invocation of the phrase is not merely informational — it is a strategic deployment of the euphemism within a culture where menstrual impurity had specific legal implications (cf. Lev 15:20). Rendering the phrase directly as "I'm having my period" (PNEUMA) strips the cultural layering and the irony of Rachel's use of ritual categories to protect stolen ritual objects. LOGOS's "the manner of women" is acceptable but slightly flatter than "the way of women" — the Hebrew *derekh* ("way, path") carries more weight than "manner." The council follows EMET. The verse is flagged ⚠️ for the register-choice divergence, documented in the Disagreement Log.

---

### On 31:36 — "Jacob Was Angry" (*wayyihar lĕYa'aqov*)

EMET renders: "Jacob was angry." LOGOS renders: "Jacob was angry." PNEUMA renders: "Jacob was furious."

The Hebrew *וַיִּחַר לְיַעֲקֹב* uses the idiom of burning anger (lit. "it burned to Jacob"). EMET and LOGOS render "angry"; PNEUMA sharpens to "furious." The council adopts "angry" (EMET and LOGOS) — the context (Jacob's extended speech of twenty years' grievance) supplies the fury without needing to front-load it in the narrative tag. The word *wayyihar* is the standard biblical idiom for strong anger (as in Cain, Gen 4:5; Esau, Gen 27:45); rendering it simply "was angry" and then allowing Jacob's speech to demonstrate the degree of that anger is the better narrative choice. PNEUMA's observation that this is Jacob's longest speech in the narrative to this point is adopted as the council's definitive note on vv. 36–42.

---

### On 31:39 — "I Bore the Loss of It" (*anokhi aḥatennah*)

EMET renders: "I bore the loss of it." LOGOS renders: "I bore the loss of it myself." PNEUMA renders: "I paid for it myself."

The Hebrew verb חָטָא (*khata'*) in Piel (*'ăkhattennah*) here means "to make compensation, to pay a penalty" — not the common meaning "to sin." All three translators agree on the compensatory sense. The council adopts "I bore the loss of it myself" (LOGOS) — adding "myself" (which is implied by *anokhi*, the emphatic first-person pronoun) makes the emphatic self-assumption of liability clear in English. EMET's version without "myself" is accurate but slightly less emphatic. PNEUMA's "I paid for it" is natural but loses the specific pastoral-liability register of Jacob's speech.

---

### On 31:40 — "The Frost" (*qeraḥ*)

EMET renders: "the frost by night." LOGOS renders: "the cold by night." PNEUMA renders: "froze in the cold at night."

The Hebrew קֶרַח (*qeraḥ*) means "frost, ice, freezing cold" — more specific than generic "cold." The council adopts "the frost by night" (EMET) — "frost" is the more precise English equivalent of *qeraḥ* and captures the specific meteorological reality of highland pasture nights in the ancient Near East. "Cold" (LOGOS) is accurate but less vivid; "froze in the cold" (PNEUMA) adds a verbal element not in the Hebrew.

---

### On 31:42 — "The Fear of Isaac" (*Pachad Yitschaq*) ⚠️

EMET renders: "the Fear of Isaac." LOGOS renders: "the Fear of Isaac." PNEUMA renders: "the Fear of Isaac."

All three translators are unanimous: "the Fear of Isaac." The divine epithet פַּחַד יִצְחָק (*Pakhad Yitschaq*) appears only at vv. 42 and 53 in the entire Hebrew Bible. The primary interpretation — "the Fear of Isaac," i.e., the awe-inspiring God before whom Isaac trembles — is the natural reading of the Hebrew word *pakhad* and is confirmed by the LXX Vaticanus (*ho phobos Isaak*, "the Fear of Isaac"). The minority scholarly reading connecting *pakhad* to an alternate root meaning "kinsman" or "thigh" is noted by EMET and PNEUMA. The council adopts "the Fear of Isaac" (unanimous), following the MT vocalization and the LXX confirmation. EMET's note on the two principal interpretations is adopted as the council's definitive scholarly statement; the verse is flagged ⚠️ for the ongoing philological debate, documented in the Disagreement Log.

On "rebuked": all three translators use "rebuked" for the final clause *wayyôkhaḥ* ("he rebuked you last night"). This is adopted unanimously — the word *yākaḥ* (Hiphil) means "to reprove, to rebuke, to adjudicate against" — capturing both God's role as witness and as enforcer of Jacob's cause.

---

### On 31:43 — "The Sons They Have Borne" (*asher yaledu*)

EMET renders: "that they have fathered." LOGOS renders: "whom they have borne." PNEUMA renders: "the children they have borne."

The Hebrew *אֲשֶׁר יָלָדוּ* is "whom/that they bore/gave birth to" — a feminine plural verb referring to the daughters' children. LOGOS's "whom they have borne" is the most precise rendering (preserving the relative clause and the maternal verb). The council adopts LOGOS's formulation. EMET's "that they have fathered" appears to be a slip — *yāladū* is the giving-birth verb, used of the mothers, not the fathers. The council corrects this: "the sons they have borne."

---

### On 31:46 — "Upon the Heap" (eating location)

EMET renders: "upon the heap." LOGOS renders: "by the heap." PNEUMA renders: "beside the mound."

The Hebrew *עַל-הַגָּל* ("upon/at the heap") is the spatial reference for the covenant meal. The council adopts "upon the heap" (EMET) — the preposition *'al* ("upon, at, by") most naturally reads as "upon" or "at" in this context. "By the heap" (LOGOS) is acceptable; "beside the mound" (PNEUMA) uses a synonym ("mound" for "heap") that the council has not adopted as the consistent rendering of *gal* (which is consistently "heap" across vv. 46–52).

---

### On 31:47 — Jegar-sahadutha and Gal-ed ⚠️

EMET renders: "Jegar-sahadutha" (Aramaic) and "Gal-ed" (Hebrew). LOGOS renders: "Jegar-sahadutha" and "Galeed." PNEUMA renders: "Jegar-sahadutha" and "Galeed."

The bilingual naming — Laban's Aramaic *Yĕgar Sāhĕdûthā'* ("heap of witness" in Aramaic) and Jacob's Hebrew *Gal'ed* ("heap of witness" in Hebrew) — is one of the most celebrated linguistic moments in the Pentateuch. Both names carry identical meaning in their respective languages. All three translators transliterate both names. The council adopts "Jegar-sahadutha" (unanimous) for the Aramaic name. For the Hebrew name, the council adopts "Gal-ed" (EMET) rather than "Galeed" (LOGOS and PNEUMA) — "Gal-ed" preserves the two constituent parts of the name (*gal* + *'ed*) more visibly in English, allowing the reader to perceive the same "heap-witness" structure as the Aramaic name alongside it, and matches the form used in the continuing narrative of vv. 48–52. The LXX Vaticanus renders only one name (*Bounos tēs martyrias*, "Hill of Witness"), losing the bilingual contrast. The council follows the MT's bilingual naming as a significant narrative feature. The verse is flagged ⚠️ for the MT/LXX divergence (LXX omits the Aramaic name) and for the "Gal-ed" vs. "Galeed" orthographic choice, documented in the Disagreement Log.

---

### On 31:49 — Mizpah (*hammitspah*)

All three translators: "Mizpah." The name הַמִּצְפָּה (*hammitspah*, "the Watchpost/Watchtower") is transliterated by all three as "Mizpah." The LXX Vaticanus translates rather than transliterates: *Skopia* ("the Watchtower/Lookout"), preserving the etymological force in Greek. The council follows all three translators: "Mizpah" as the transliterated form, with the etymology ("May the LORD watch between you and me") embedded in the verse itself. PNEUMA's observation on the Mizpah saying is adopted by the council: in its original context, this is a covenant-enforcement formula, not a blessing for departing friends. God is the divine surveillance agent watching over two parties who cannot trust each other when separated — it is a warning as much as a prayer. The council's translation preserves this precisely as it stands, without editorial softening.

---

### On 31:53 — "Judge Between Us" — Plural Verb (*yishpetu*) ⚠️

EMET renders: "shall judge between us" (noting plural force). LOGOS renders: "judge between us" (preserving plural). PNEUMA renders: "judge between us."

The verb יִשְׁפְּטוּ (*yishpetu*) is grammatically plural — "they shall judge" — despite the subject being "the God of Abraham and the God of Nahor, the God of their father." The plural verb is the *lectio difficilior* (harder reading) and is retained in the MT against the Samaritan Pentateuch's singular harmonization. The LXX Vaticanus reads *krinai ho theos* (singular — "may God judge"), harmonizing toward monotheistic expectation.

The council adopts "judge between us" (all three translators agree on the wording, if not on the grammatical marking). The council preserves the plural force of the MT — the English "judge" without a subject can be read as either singular or plural, maintaining ambiguity appropriate to the theological tension of the verse. Jacob's immediate response — swearing only by the Fear of his father Isaac (v. 53b) — implicitly corrects Laban's compound invocation: Jacob refuses to swear by Nahor's God, swearing only by the one he knows. The verse is flagged ⚠️ for the grammatically plural verb and the MT/LXX divergence, documented in the Disagreement Log.

---

### On 31:54 — "Offered a Sacrifice" (*wayyizbakh zevakh*)

All three translators: "offered a sacrifice on the mountain." The Hebrew *וַיִּזְבַּח יַעֲקֹב זֶבַח* (cognate accusative: "he sacrificed a sacrifice") is the covenant-sealing act. The council adopts "offered a sacrifice on the mountain" (unanimous). The LXX Vaticanus reads *ethysen Iakob thysian en tō orei* — exact rendering. The following clause "eat bread" (*le'ekhol-lekhem*) is the covenant meal that ratifies the agreement; the council renders it "eat bread" throughout, following all three translators. LOGOS's note on the covenantal significance of the shared meal in the Hebrew scriptures (cf. Gen 26:30; Ex 24:11) is adopted as the council's definitive contextual statement.

---

### On 31:55 — "His Grandchildren" (*banav*) and Versification Note

EMET renders: "his sons and his daughters." LOGOS renders: "his grandchildren and his daughters." PNEUMA renders: "his grandchildren and his daughters."

The Hebrew *לְבָנָיו וְלִבְנֹתָיו* ("his sons and his daughters") refers in context to Jacob's children (Laban's grandchildren) and Rachel and Leah. The council adopts "his grandchildren and his daughters" (LOGOS and PNEUMA) — the contextual referents are unambiguous, and "grandchildren" accurately identifies the relationship without confusion. At this final moment (v. 55), Laban is saying goodbye; the reference is clearly to Jacob's children, Laban's grandchildren, and to Laban's daughters Rachel and Leah. The LXX Vaticanus reads *tous huious autou kai tas thygateras autou* ("his sons and his daughters"), following the literal Hebrew. The council prefers the contextually accurate "grandchildren" over the formally literal "sons."

**Versification Note:** In the Masoretic Text (and in this translation), v. 55 is the final verse of Genesis 31 (55 verses total). In the LXX versification and some traditions, this verse appears as Genesis 32:1. The content is identical across traditions; only the chapter-verse assignment differs. This translation follows MT versification throughout.

---

### The Theology of Genesis 31 — Flight, Confrontation, and Covenant

The council adopts a shared observation from all three translators as its definitive theological framing: Genesis 31 is the chapter where divine providence works through human flight, deception, anger, and the fragile machinery of treaty-making. Jacob does not leave Paddan-aram cleanly — he steals away, his wife steals her father's gods, and the whole departure is colored by the chapter's repeated use of the verb *ganav* ("to steal"). Yet God commands the flight (v. 3), warns Laban in the night (v. 24), and is invoked at every point in the confrontation as the unseen arbiter. The Mizpah covenant is not a happy ending but an armed truce: God watches between two men who cannot trust each other.

PNEUMA's observation on Rachel and Leah's speech (vv. 14–16) is adopted: this is one of the most striking speeches by women in Genesis. Their grievance against Laban is not passive — they have been sold, their purchase price consumed, their inheritance foreclosed. When they say "do whatever God has told you," it is not submission but active endorsement born of their own analysis of their situation. The narrator gives them the last word before the flight begins.

EMET's observation on the *ganav* wordplay throughout the chapter is adopted as the council's definitive literary statement: the verb "to steal" appears in rapid succession across vv. 19–20 (Rachel steals teraphim; Jacob steals Laban's heart), recurs in Laban's accusation (vv. 26–27, 30), and surfaces in Jacob's own unwitting death sentence over the thief (v. 32). The family that deceives is caught in its own web of deception. The narrator does not moralize; the irony is structural.

---

## ⚠️ Disagreement Log

### 31:1 — *Kavod*: "Wealth" (Consensus) vs. "Glory" (EMET)

**The issue:** Whether the Hebrew *כָּבוֹד* (*kavod*) in Laban's sons' complaint ("he has made all this *kavod*") should be rendered "wealth" or "glory."

**EMET's position:** Render "glory" — *kavod* carries both the sense of material wealth and the honor/renown attached to it; the LXX Vaticanus reads *tēn doxan tautēn* ("this glory"), preserving the same word *doxa*. Rendering "glory" keeps the full weight of the word and the rhetorical irony (the sons bitterly describing Jacob's prosperity as "glory").

**LOGOS's position:** Render "wealth" — in context, the sons are describing Jacob's material accumulation, not his divine or royal honor; "wealth" makes this clear without requiring annotation.

**PNEUMA's position:** Render "all this wealth he has built up" — "wealth" is the primary meaning in context; the added "built up" reflects the narrative arc of Jacob's accumulation.

**LXX Vaticanus:** *tēn doxan tautēn* — "this glory" — supporting the "glory" reading.

**SOPHIA's resolution:** The council adopts "wealth" (LOGOS) for the consensus translation. While EMET's "glory" preserves the full *kavod* range and LXX support, "wealth" is the contextually dominant sense and reads naturally for the English reader without requiring a note to unpack the irony. The "glory/wealth" double meaning and the LXX support are documented here for scholars consulting the Disagreement Log.

---

### 31:7 / 31:41 — *Aseret Monim*: "Ten Times" (MT) vs. "Ten Female Lambs" (LXX)

**The issue:** The MT reads עֲשֶׂרֶת מֹנִים (*'aseret monim*, "ten times") for Laban's wage-changes at both v. 7 and v. 41. The LXX Vaticanus reads *deka amnas* (δέκα ἄμνας, "ten female lambs") — a striking divergence apparently reading *monim* as a livestock measure rather than a numeral multiplier.

**EMET's position:** Follow the MT — "ten times." The Leningrad Codex is clear; *monim* as "times/counts" is the more natural reading of the MT, and the parallel phrase in Numbers 14:22 ("you have tested me these ten times") confirms the idiomatic use of "ten" as a round number for repeated action.

**LOGOS's position:** Follow the MT — "ten times." The LXX reading "ten female lambs" does not align with the narrative (Laban changed the species of animal, not paid Jacob ten lambs). The LXX may reflect a different Vorlage or a translator's interpretation of *monim* as a weight/measure.

**PNEUMA's position:** Follow the MT — "ten times."

**LXX Vaticanus:** *deka amnas* — "ten female lambs" — both occurrences. Unanimously rejected by all three council translators in favor of the MT.

**SOPHIA's resolution:** The council unanimously follows the MT's "ten times" at both v. 7 and v. 41. The LXX variant "ten female lambs" is documented here as the most significant MT/LXX divergence in Genesis 31. The LXX reading is unlikely to preserve an older Vorlage; it appears to reflect either a different manuscript tradition or an interpretive rendering of *monim* as a livestock measure. The internal consistency of the narrative (Jacob's wage-type changed repeatedly, not paid in fixed quantities) supports "ten times" as the reading that coheres with the preceding narrative.

---

### 31:13 — *Ha-El Bet-El*: "God of Bethel" (MT/Consensus) vs. "God who appeared to you in the place of God" (LXX)

**The issue:** The MT divine self-identification *anokhi ha-El Bet-El* ("I am the God of Bethel") is terse and place-name-specific. The LXX Vaticanus reads *egō eimi ho Theos ho ophtheis soi en topō Theou* ("I am the God who appeared to you in the place of God") — an expansion that translates "Bethel" interpretively as "the place of God" and adds a relative clause identifying the Bethel theophany.

**All three translators:** Follow the MT — "the God of Bethel." Unanimous.

**SOPHIA's resolution:** The council unanimously follows the MT. The LXX's expansion is interpretively interesting (it renders "Bethel" = "house of God" as "place of God" and makes the theophany reference explicit) but the MT's terse "God of Bethel" is the more historically significant reading. The specific place-name invocation ("Bethel") calls up Jacob's experience in Genesis 28 with greater concision than the LXX's relative clause. The council notes that the LXX reading avoids the place-name — possibly to sidestep the theological problem of a divine self-identification tied to a specific location. The MT form is the *lectio difficilior*.

---

### 31:20 — "Stole the Heart": Idiom Preservation vs. Dynamic Rendering

**The issue:** Whether the Hebrew idiom *ganav et-lev* ("stole the heart" = "deceived by concealment") should be rendered literally (EMET, LOGOS) or paraphrased for clarity (PNEUMA).

**EMET's position:** Preserve the literal idiom: "Jacob stole the heart of Laban the Aramean."

**LOGOS's position:** Same as EMET — preserve the idiom.

**PNEUMA's position:** Restructure to explain the idiom: "Jacob slipped away without telling Laban — he stole the heart of Laban the Aramean, giving him no warning that he was leaving." Leads with the act of concealment before stating the idiom, making the meaning clear without losing the Hebrew phrase.

**SOPHIA's resolution:** The council adopts the EMET/LOGOS rendering: "Jacob stole the heart of Laban the Aramean, in that he did not tell him that he was fleeing." The idiom is preserved first, then explained by the subordinate clause that follows — exactly as the Hebrew does it. The reader who does not immediately grasp "stole the heart" is given the explanation within the same sentence structure as the original. PNEUMA's restructuring, while thoughtful, inverts the sequence and dilutes the literary effect of the word *ganav* falling three times in rapid succession across vv. 19–20. The LXX Vaticanus preserves the idiom exactly.

---

### 31:24 / 31:29 — *Mittov 'Ad-Ra'*: "Either Good or Bad" vs. "Either Good or Evil"

**The issue:** The merism *mittov 'ad-ra'* ("from good to bad/evil") — whether *ra'* is best rendered "bad" or "evil" in English.

**EMET's position:** "Either good or evil" — *ra'* includes moral badness and the full range of harmful speech.

**LOGOS's position:** "Either good or bad" — *ra'* here means simply "harmful" rather than specifically morally evil; "bad" is the more natural English word for the polar opposite of "good" in everyday speech.

**PNEUMA's position:** "Not good, not bad" — restructuring the merism as a negation. Captures the meaning but loses the "from... to" merism structure.

**SOPHIA's resolution:** The council adopts "either good or bad" (LOGOS) at both v. 24 and v. 29. "Bad" is the more natural English pairing with "good" in this kind of idiomatic merism. "Evil" (EMET) carries too strong a moral-wickedness connotation in English for what the Hebrew *ra'* means here — *ra'* simply means "bad, harmful, harmful speech." The LXX's *apo tou kalou heōs tou ponērou* ("from the good to the evil") confirms the merism structure; the council preserves the "either... or" rendering (LOGOS) over PNEUMA's negation. The rendering is identical at v. 24 and v. 29 for consistency.

---

### 31:35 — *Derekh Nashim*: "The Way of Women" (EMET/Consensus) vs. "I'm Having My Period" (PNEUMA)

**The issue:** Whether Rachel's euphemistic phrase *derekh nashim* ("the way of women") should be preserved as a euphemism in the translation (EMET, LOGOS) or rendered as a direct statement of the meaning (PNEUMA).

**EMET's position:** "The way of women is upon me" — the euphemism must be preserved; Rachel's strategic use of ritual-impurity categories is part of the narrative's meaning. "The way of women" is also the unique biblical idiom, attested only here in this exact form.

**LOGOS's position:** "The manner of women is upon me" — slightly softer than EMET's "way" but preserves the euphemism.

**PNEUMA's position:** "I'm having my period" — renders the referent directly; in a modern English text, the euphemism requires explanation while the direct statement is immediately clear. The narrative's irony (ritual impurity protecting ritual objects) can be noted but the translation itself should communicate.

**LXX Vaticanus:** *ta kata tas gynaikas* ("the things according to [the custom of] women") — preserves the euphemism.

**SOPHIA's resolution:** The council adopts "the way of women is upon me" (EMET). The primary reasons: (1) the euphemism *derekh nashim* is Rachel's chosen register, and her choice of register is part of the narrative — she is invoking specific cultural and legal categories, not merely stating a biological fact; (2) the LXX Vaticanus confirms the euphemistic rendering; (3) the phrase's uniqueness in the Hebrew Bible (it appears only here in this exact form) signals that the narrator is doing something deliberate with the language; (4) "the way of women" is not opaque in English — an English reader immediately understands the reference. LOGOS's "the manner of women" is also acceptable; the council prefers EMET's "the way" as a more direct rendering of *derekh*. PNEUMA's objection is noted and documented here.

---

### 31:42 / 31:53 — *Pachad Yitschaq*: "The Fear of Isaac" — Philological Debate

**The issue:** Whether *pachad* in the divine epithet *Pachad Yitschaq* means "Fear/Terror" (standard reading) or derives from an alternate root meaning "Kinsman/Thigh" (minority scholarly reading).

**EMET's position:** "The Fear of Isaac" — the natural reading of *pakhad* throughout the Hebrew Bible is "fear, terror, dread." The LXX Vaticanus reads *ho phobos Isaak* ("the Fear of Isaac"), confirming the standard reading. The two-interpretation debate is noted but the translation follows the MT vocalization and the LXX.

**LOGOS's position:** "The Fear of Isaac" — same reasoning. Notes the scholarly minority reading (Albright, Cross) but follows the standard lexical meaning.

**PNEUMA's position:** "The Fear of Isaac" — same. Notes that Isaac's experience at the Akedah (Genesis 22) is the likely context for this epithet: the God before whom Isaac lay on the altar left a permanent mark of holy terror.

**LXX Vaticanus:** *ho phobos Isaak* — "the Fear of Isaac" — unanimous support for the standard reading.

**SOPHIA's resolution:** The council is unanimous: "the Fear of Isaac" at both v. 42 and v. 53. The philological debate about the alternate "Kinsman of Isaac" reading (based on comparison with Arabic *fakhidh*, "thigh") is noted here for scholars. The standard reading of *pakhad* as "fear/terror" is supported by: (1) the MT vocalization; (2) the LXX Vaticanus translation; (3) the immediate contextual emphasis on God's terrifying holiness (v. 42: God saw Jacob's affliction and rebuked Laban; the "Fear" who vindicated Jacob is presented as an awe-inspiring power). The minority scholarly reading, while philologically possible, requires a departure from the natural sense of the word and lacks manuscript support. The verse is flagged ⚠️ for the ongoing scholarly debate.

---

### 31:47 — "Gal-ed" (EMET/Consensus) vs. "Galeed" (LOGOS, PNEUMA)

**The issue:** Whether the Hebrew place-name גַּלְעֵד should be rendered "Gal-ed" (EMET) or "Galeed" (LOGOS, PNEUMA) in English transliteration.

**EMET's position:** "Gal-ed" — preserving the two constituent parts (*gal* = heap; *'ed* = witness) visually in English, allowing the reader to perceive the same "heap-witness" structure as the Aramaic Jegar-sahadutha.

**LOGOS's position:** "Galeed" — follows the conventional English transliteration of the place-name in most formal English translations and in subsequent biblical geography (Gilead).

**PNEUMA's position:** "Galeed" — same as LOGOS.

**SOPHIA's resolution:** The council adopts "Gal-ed" (EMET). The bilingual naming verse (v. 47) is precisely about the identical meaning of the two names in two languages. Rendering the Hebrew name as "Gal-ed" makes the two-part structure (*gal* + *'ed*) visible in the same way that "Jegar-sahadutha" preserves the Aramaic form — the reader can see both names as two-part compounds meaning "heap-of-witness." "Galeed" merges the parts into a single opaque proper noun. The name Gilead (used in the broader geographic sense) will develop from this Gal-ed; the council uses "Gal-ed" consistently across vv. 47–52.

**Additional MT/LXX note:** The LXX Vaticanus renders only a single name (*Bounos tēs martyrias*, "Hill of Witness") for both Laban's Aramaic name and Jacob's Hebrew name — collapsing the bilingual contrast that is the narrative's entire point at this verse. The MT's preservation of both distinct names is a significant narrative feature; the council follows the MT.

---

### 31:53 — Plural Verb *Yishpetu*: "Judge" (Plural MT) vs. "May God Judge" (Singular LXX)

**The issue:** The MT uses the plural verb יִשְׁפְּטוּ (*yishpetu*, "they shall judge") in v. 53, despite the subject apparently being a single divine entity (or two divine names treated as one). The Samaritan Pentateuch and LXX Vaticanus read the singular.

**EMET's position:** Preserve the MT plural — render "shall judge" with a note on the grammatical plurality. The plural is the *lectio difficilior*; it may reflect Laban's polytheistic framework (God of Abraham and God of Nahor as distinct parties) or a compound subject construction.

**LOGOS's position:** Render "judge between us" — the English "judge" is grammatically unmarked for number and preserves the MT's plurality without forcing a decision.

**PNEUMA's position:** "Judge between us" — same as LOGOS. Notes the plural verb as a possible remnant of older polytheistic framing; observes that Jacob pointedly responds by swearing only by the Fear of Isaac, not by Nahor's God.

**LXX Vaticanus:** *krinai ho theos* — "may God judge" — singular, harmonizing toward monotheism. The LXX thereby removes the grammatical tension of the MT.

**SOPHIA's resolution:** The council adopts "judge between us" (LOGOS and PNEUMA). The English "judge" preserves the ambiguity of the MT's plural without either forcing a singular reading (against the MT) or rendering it explicitly as "they shall judge" (which would require explanation for an English reader). The MT plural *yishpetu* is documented here as a significant textual feature: it may reflect the compound subject (God of Abraham + God of Nahor = plural), Laban's polytheistic framework, or an ancient formula that preserves older theological categories. Jacob's immediate response — swearing only by the Fear of Isaac, not by Nahor's God — draws the distinction the text has left in tension. The council is substantially agreed; the verse is flagged ⚠️ for the grammatical plurality issue and the MT/LXX divergence.
